channel.title: Landscape design on xpsong
channel.description: Recent content in Landscape design on xpsong

item.title: Analysing spatial patterns of the landscape
item.description:
In landscape ecology, we study interactions between organisms and their surrounding environment. The physical landscape is often quantified using “landscape metrics”, for further analyses alongside other sources of data.

My recent lectures are focused on introducing such concepts to architects and designers. While I’ve used FRAGSTATS and other GIS software for teaching in the past, I’ve always struggled with compatibility/licensing issues that just made the whole process really cumbersome.

Recently, I came across this R package called landscapemetrics. I was absolutely blown away by the sheer power of this thing. I always wanted to be able to introduce people to spatial analysis in R, and to show how a seamless, reproducible workflow can make all your problems disappear. This was the tipping point for me… I decided to give it a shot!

This post provides a quick preview of the workshop content. The workshop is meant to be a follow-up lesson to the Introduction to R series I’ll be conducting next semester.

Workshop outline:


  1. Why analyse spatial patterns?

  2. Landscape ecology: Conceptual models

  3. Land cover classification

  4. Landscape metrics


I’ve uploaded the slides and data on Github, so that anyone interested to try out the analysis for themselves will be able to do so. If you’re interested in geospatial analysis, check it out!



Slide deck:

Note: Click on the slides and use arrows to navigate

item.title: An ecosystem service design loop for using vegetation to mitigate the urban heat island effect
Ecosystem services in urban landscapes
item.description:
Urban vegetation and green spaces provide cities with a number of benefits which are known as ‘ecosystem services’. These include reducing flood risk, cooling urban areas, and providing spaces for recreation.

Most research on urban ecosystem services has been conducted in temperate Europe and North America, but we know relatively little about how tropical urban areas can be designed to enhance ecosystem service provision. Tropical areas, particularly Southeast Asia, are rapidly becoming more urban, with cities growing larger and people living in higher densities. It is therefore important to better understand how urban greenery can contribute to enhancing the sustainability of such cities.

The main aim of this project is to use new types of data and modelling approaches to improve urban landscape design and obtain the greatest value from ecosystem services. Physical, ecological and socio-cultural parameters are used as inputs to model various types of ecosystem services. The landscape is designed using 3D point cloud data, which produces a representation of the landscape that is highly accurate. The iterative process of model simulation and design feedback provides opportunities to improve designs based on scientific results.
